docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This C++ library provides simple cryptographic API with focus on RSA and AES. Built on OpenSSL, but isn't a direct wrapper to make the usage as simple as possible.

This library is MIT licensed, so you can use it however you want in your projects!

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• C++ Standard : C++11 or later
  • Dependencies : OpenSSL3.0+

docs.title: Example usage
docs.text:
Example encryption: 

#include "tancrypt.hpp"

int main()
{
  // Databuffer from string
  dutils::dbuffer payload("Hewwo, I am secret ^.^");

  // Keypair with key size of 2048 gets generated and our buffer gets encrypted
  tancrypt::RSA::pkic key;
  key.generate_keypair(2048);
  dutils::dbuffer res = tancrypt::RSA::encrypt(key,payload);

  // Check results compared original, hex x  encrypted
  std::cout << "Original:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << my_message.toStr() << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Hex:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << dutils::hexStr(payload) << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Encrypted:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << dutils::hexStr(res) << std::endl;

  return 0;
}

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This namespace provides basic AES cryptographic operations, depends AES::keyc. For proper use, also check documentation of AES::keyc.

Example usage: 

int main()
{
  dutils::dbuffer payload("Hewwo, I am secret >.<");

  // Makes life easier as it shortens tancrypt::AES --> AES:: 
  using namespace tancrypt;

  // Setting up symmetric key - key is not padded/prehashed/derived, so keyc's hashing will need to be used
  dutils::dbuffer my_keydata("Hewwo, I am key ^.^");

  // We enabled hashing here by adding hashAlg::SHA256
  tancrypt::AES::keyc key_variant2(my_keydata,AES::Type::CBC256,hashAlg::SHA256);
  dutils::dbuffer enc_buffer = AES::encrypt(key_variant2, payload);

  std::cout << "Original: " << payload.toStr() << std::endl; 
  std::cout << "Original(hex): " << dutils::hexStr(payload) << std::endl; 
  std::cout << "Encrypted(hex): " << dutils::hexStr(enc_buffer) << std::endl;

  dutils::dbuffer dec_buffer = AES::decrypt(key_variant2, enc_buffer);

  std::cout << "Decrypted(hex): " << dutils::hexStr(dec_buffer) << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Decrypted: " << dec_buffer.data() << std::endl;

  return 0;  
}

docs.title: AES::encrypt(AES::keyc &key_container, dutils::dbuffer &buffer)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • tancrypt::AES::keyc &key_container - AES key with preloaded credentials
    • dutils::dbuffer &buffer - Data to encrypt
  • Returns:
    • dutils::dbuffer AES_DATA - Encrypted buffer

docs.title: AES::decrypt(AES::keyc &key_container, dutils::dbuffer &buffer)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • tancrypt::AES::keyc &key_container - AES key with preloaded credentials
    • dutils::dbuffer &buffer - Data to decrypt
  • Returns:
    • dutils::dbuffer AES_DATA - Decrypted buffer

Please note that this should be only used for ciphers where it's applicable, otherwise logic error exception "Not applicable" is thrown.

docs.title: Dutils
docs.text: Helpful utilities, provides custom data buffer and data utility functions.

docs.title: dutils::dbuffer
docs.text: Useful class for storing data it's based on std::vector<unsigned char>, main advantage is that dutils::dbuffer provides easy conversion from string and back.

docs.title: dutils::dbuffer constructors
docs.text: Dbuffer supports various construction methods.

docs.title: dutils::dbuffer(std::initializer_list<unsinged int> list)
docs.text:
Equivalent to std::vector<unsigned char> buffer = {1,2,3...};

  • Parameters:
    • std::initializer_list<unsigned int> list - Input data
  • Returns:
    • dutils::dbuffer buffer

docs.title: dutils::dbuffer(std::vector<unsigned char> dbuffer)
docs.text:
Copies data from std::vector<unsigned char> to newly created blank dbuffer, this is primarily for compatibility purposes, but direct construction of dutils::dbuffer is more efficient.

  • Parameters:
    • std::vector<unsinged char> buffer - Buffer to copy
  • Returns:
    • dutils::dbuffer buffer

docs.title: dutils::dbuffer::toStr()
docs.text:
Warning

Please note that the dbuffer contents are not checked for string safety, thus when converting you must handle possible nullterminators yourself.

Converts dbuffer contents to string

  • Parametrs:
  • Returns:
    • std::string buffer_stringified

docs.title: dutils::hexStr
docs.text: This function provides an easy way to convert data into string interpretation of hex values. Useful when you want to print out or compare buffers with non-string data.

docs.title: dutils::hexStr(const dutils::dbuffer &data)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • const dutils::dbuffer &data - The input data buffer.
  • Returns:
    • std::string - A formatted hex string (16 blocks per row).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Algorithm support

The supported OpenSSL algorithms are limited to those mapped in enum class hashAlg. This prevents errors from requesting unsupported or non-existent algorithms.

This component provides the hashing functionality required for RSA::sign and RSA::verify, but it can also be used as a standalone hasher. Supported algorithms are directly provided by OpenSSL.

Extends the namespace pkicxx without a sub-namespace.

docs.title: enum class tancrypt::hashAlg : int
docs.text: This enum class is used as hashAlg type, it allows better type safety and decreases amount of errors caused by typos

docs.title: Available algorithms
docs.text:
• hashAlg::MDC2
  • hashAlg::MD4
  • hashAlg::MD5
  • hashAlg::SHA1
  • hashAlg::RSA_SHA1
  • hashAlg::SHA224
  • hashAlg::SHA256
  • hashAlg::SHA384
  • hashAlg::SHA512
  • hashAlg::SHA512_224
  • hashAlg::SHA512_256
  • hashAlg::SM3
  • hashAlg::BLAKE2B512
  • hashAlg::BLAKE2S256

docs.title: tancrypt::hash
docs.text: Provides hashing functionality required for sign and verify functions, but can be also used independently.

docs.title: tancrypt::hash(dutils::dbuffer& data,hashAlg alg)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • dutils::dbuffer& data - Data buffer to hash
    • tancrypt::hashAlg alg - Algorithm to use for the digest.
  • Returns:
    • dutils::dbuffer data - Hashed data buffer

docs.title: _hashTypeMap
docs.text: Internal function that returns internal std::map, which maps enums to the OpenSSL algorithm names

docs.title: static const std::map<int,const char>& _hashTypeMap()
docs.text:
• Parameters:
  • Returns:
    • static const std::map<int,const char*>& - Map of enum : int values to char* names of the algorithms

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Warning

Please note that when constructing without a Hashing enabled on the keyc, you're expected to provide a properly padded key of correct length. If you do not want to handle your key enable hashing by constructing with hashAlg.

AES::keyc fulfills function of key container, similar to how RSA::pkic does, the AES::keyc stores a key and AES type.

docs.title: AES::keyc()
docs.text:
• Parameters:

Constructs blank keyc, you then need to manually setup the key container. When using hashing via the keyc, you also need to enable the hashing not just set the hashAlg.

Example of blank construction: 

// Blank key created
tancrypt::AES::keyc key;

// Setting AES type 
key.setType(tancrypt::AES::CBC256);

// Setting key assuming buffer is a key of proper size
// Proper size can be acquired after setting the AES type via getCiphKLen().
int key_len = key.getCiphKLen();
key.setKey(buffer);

// In case we do not have proper key in advance we can use built in hashing

// Blank key created
tancrypt::AES::keyc key;

// Setting AES type 
key.setType(type);

// Setting the key assuming arbitrary key data
key.setKey(buffer);

// Setting hashing algorithm, though hashAlg::SHA256 is default
key.setHashAlg(hashAlg::SHA256);

// We must also enable hashing, otherwise it won't be used
key.setHashEnabled(true);

docs.title: AES::keyc(const dutils::dbuffer& key,AES::Type type)
docs.text:
When constructing without hashAlg, you must ensure your key data are prepadded

  • Parameters:
    • const dutils::dbuffer& key - Prepadded key data
    • tancrypt::AES::Type type - Type of AES algorithm to use
  • Returns:
    • tancrypt::AES::keyc key

docs.title: AES::keyc(const dutils::dbuffer& key,AES::Type type,hashAlg alg)
docs.text:
Note

Bear in mind that to avoid possible race conditions and unnecessary state holding, the hashing occurs on encryption runtime this may cause this key variant to be less efficient.

  • Parameters:
    • dutils::dbuffer key - Key data
    • tancrypt::AES::Type type - AES algorithm type
    • hashAlg alg - Hashing algorithm to use on key during encryption
  • Returns:
    • tancrypt::AES::keyc key

docs.title: AES supported types
docs.text:
• AES::Type::CBC128 - AES-128-CBC
  • AES::Type::CBC192 - AES-192-CBC
  • AES::Type::CBC256 - AES-256-CBC
  • AES::Type::GCM128 - AES-128-GCM
  • AES::Type::GCM192 - AES-192-GCM
  • AES::Type::GCM256 - AES-256-GCM

docs.title: AES::key::setType(AES::Type type)
docs.text:
Sets type of AES algorithm, does not corrupt key and can be used even past first initialization

  • Parameters:

  • Returns:

  • Parameters:

  • Returns:

Sets the key data

  • Parameters:

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Note

Please note that newly created instance of RSA::pkic is blank and needs to be initialized properly before it can be used.

PKIC serves as a container, which stores and manages the RSA keys. The PKIC by itself does not provide any cryptographic operations, it only provides the keys. class pkic extends the namespace RSA and is automatically included in the RSA header.

If you wish to merely generate keypairs or load keypairs and manage them, you can include tancrypt-pkic.hpp as a standalone header.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Sub-namespace tancrypt::RSA provides basic cryptographic functions and pulls in tancrypt::RSA::pkic as required dependency.

All provided functions are provided as static stateless functions, this simplifies their usage.

docs.title: RSA::encrypt(pkic& key,dutils::dbuffer& payload)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • tancrypt::RSA::pkic key - Key container (priv/pubkey must be loaded)
    • dutils::dbuffer& payload - Data buffer to encrypt
  • Returns:
    • dutils::dbuffer - Encrypted data buffer

docs.title: Encrypt example
docs.text:
#include "tancrypt.hpp"

int main()
{
  // Keypair generation
  tancrypt::RSA::pkic key;
  key.generate_keypair(2048);

  // Preparing example buffer from string
  dutils::dbuffer payload("Hewwo, I am secret ^.^");

  // Encrypting data
  dutils::dbuffer res = tancrypt::RSA::encrypt(key,payload);

  // Debug write into console to see the results
  std::cout << "Original:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << payload.toStr()<< std::endl;
  std::cout << "Hex::" << std::endl;
  std::cout << dutils::hexStr(payload) << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Encrypted:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << dutils::hexStr(res) << std::endl;

  return 0;
}

docs.title: RSA::decrypt(pkic& key,dutils::dbuffer& payload)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • tancrypt::RSA::pkic& key - Key container (Must contain private key)
    • dutils::dbuffer& payload - Encrypted data buffer
  • Returns:
    • dutils::dbuffer - Decrypted data buffer

docs.title: Decrypt example
docs.text:
#include "tancrypt.hpp"

int main()
{
  // Generating keypair
  tancrypt::RSA::pkic key;
  key.generate_keypair(2048);

  // Preparing example data
  dutils::dbuffer payload("Hewwo, I am secret ^.^");

  // Encrypting the data
  dutils::dbuffer res = tancrypt::RSA::encrypt(key,payload);

  // Debug log into console - original unencrypted
  std::cout << "Original:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << payload.toStr() << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Hex:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << dutils::hexStr(payload) << std::endl;

  // Debug log into console - after encrypting  
  std::cout << "Encrypted:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << dutils::hexStr(res) << std::endl;


  // Decrypting the data
  dutils::dbuffer res_decrypted = tancrypt::RSA::decrypt(key,res);

  // Debug log into console - after decrypting
  std::cout << "Res decrypted hex:"<< std::endl;
  std::cout << dutils::hexStr(res_decrypted) << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Res decrypted:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << res_decrypted.data() << std::endl

  return 0;
}

docs.title: RSA::sign(pkic& key,dutils::dbuffer &buffer, hashAlg alg)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • tancrypt::RSA::pkic key - key container (must contain private key)
    • dutils::dbuffer &buffer - The data to be signed
    • tancrypt::hashAlg alg - Hashing algorithm to use for the digest
  • Returns:
    • dutils::dbuffer - Data signature

docs.title: Signature example
docs.text:
#include "tancrypt.hpp"

int main()
{
  tancrypt::RSA::pkic key_store;
  key_store.generate_keypair(2048);

  std::string message = "I confirm this transaction.";
  dutils::dbuffer data(message.begin(), message.end());

  // Signing the data using SHA256
  dutils::dbuffer signature = tancrypt::RSA::pki::sign(key_store, data, tancrypt::hashAlg::SHA256);

  std::cout << "Signature (Hex):" << std::endl;
  std::cout << tancrypt::hexStr(signature) << std::endl;

  return 0;
}

docs.title: RSA::verify
docs.text:
Note

Algorithm choice must match the algorithm which was used to sign the data, otherwise the operation will fail.

docs.title: RSA::verify(pkic& key, dutils::dbuffer&sig, dutils::dbuffer &buffer, hashAlg alg)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • tancrypt::RSA::pkic& key - key container (must contain public key)
    • dutils::dbuffer &sig - Singature data buffer
    • dutils::dbuffer &buffer - The data to verify
    • tancrypt::hashAlg alg - Hashing algorithm to use for the digest
  • Returns:
    • bool - Signature matches data (returns true if valid)

docs.title: Verify signature example
docs.text:
#include "tancrypt.hpp"

int main()
{
  // Assuming 'key_store' contains public key, 'signature' and 'data' contain valid data
  bool is_valid = tancrypt::RSA::pki::verify(key_store, signature, data, tancrypt::hashAlg::SHA256);

  if (is_valid) {
      std::cout << "Signature is authentic!" << std::endl;
  } else {
      std::cout << "Signature verification failed." << std::endl;
  }

  return 0;
}

docs.title: Packages
docs.text: The lib can be also acquired from following repos.

docs.title: Arch linux
docs.text: AUR: yay -S pkicxx

docs.title: Adding library as dependency
docs.text:
You can simply add it as your dependency into lib/. 

cd lib/
git clone https://github.com/Sora-3e8/tancrypt
Now either add it as subdirectory into your cmake or build manually using: 
cd lib/
git clone https://github.com/Sora-3e8/tancrypt && cd tancrypt/build
cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release .. && cmake --build .

docs.title: Installing as system lib
docs.text:
You can install the library to your system and keep it as shared lib. To install use following commands:

git clone https://github.com/Sora-3e8/tancrypt && cd tancrypt/build
cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release .. && cmake --build .
sudo cmake --install .
Now you can link against it as if it was normal system library. The library should be findable by cmake.
